meaning: so that; in order to; like; as
detailed_explanation: Expresses purpose (doing X so that Y happens) or manner (doing like/as something). N3's most versatile pattern - purpose vs manner depends on context.
conjugation_rules: Verb (dictionary/negative/nai-form) + ように / い-adjective + ように / な-adjective + な + ように / Noun + の + ように

usage_examples.japanese: 後ろの人も聞こえるように、大きい声で話してください
usage_examples.reading: うしろのひともきこえるように、おおきいこえではなしてください
usage_examples.meaning: Please speak loudly so that people in back can hear
usage_examples.nuance: Purpose - action taken to achieve result

usage_examples.japanese: 彼はまるで子供のように泣いた
usage_examples.reading: かれはまるでこどものようにないた
usage_examples.meaning: He cried just like a child

usage_examples.japanese: 忘れないように、メモしておこう
usage_examples.reading: わすれないように、めもしておこう
usage_examples.meaning: Let's make a note so we don't forget

nuance_notes: CRITICAL DISTINCTION: 〜ように (purpose) vs 〜ようにする (effort/habit). 〜ように = so that (external result). 〜ようにする = make an effort to / get into habit of. Also: 〜ようになる = come to/reach state where. Context determines: purpose (uncontrollable outcomes → ように) vs direct action (controllable → ために).

related_patterns.key_difference: 〜ために = direct controllable purpose; 〜ように = indirect/uncontrollable outcomes, softer
〜ように = so that; 〜ようにする = make effort to / establish habit
〜ようになる = come to be able to / reach state (change over time)
〜みたいに = casual 'like'; 〜ように = more formal/literary comparison

common_mistakes: MAJOR ERROR: Confusing 〜ように (purpose) with 〜ようにする (habit). '勉強するように' = so that I study (weird) vs '勉強するようにしている' = making an effort to study (correct). Also: Using ために for uncontrollable outcomes (×日本に行けるために). Use ように for potential/desire outcomes.

meaning: make an effort to; try to; get into the habit of
detailed_explanation: Habit formation or conscious effort pattern. Different from 〜ように (purpose). Focuses on speaker's intentional behavior change.

usage_examples.japanese: 毎日運動するようにしている
usage_examples.reading: まいにちうんどうするようにしている
usage_examples.meaning: I'm making an effort to exercise every day

usage_examples.japanese: ８時に寝るようにしてください
usage_examples.reading: じゅうじにねるようにしてください
usage_examples.meaning: Please try to go to bed by 10

usage_examples.japanese: 朝ごはんを食べるようにしたら、調子が良くなった
usage_examples.reading: あさごはんをたべるようにしたら、ちょうしがよくなった
usage_examples.meaning: When I made an effort to eat breakfast, my condition improved

nuance_notes: 〜ようにする = active effort/habit. 〜ようにしている = ongoing state of effort. 〜ようにした = made effort (past). 〜ようにしてください = soft request. CONTRAST with 〜ことにする (decision/commitment - more definitive) and 〜ようになる (natural change, not effort).

related_patterns.key_difference: 〜ようにする = effort/trying; 〜ことにする = definitive decision/commitment
〜ようにする = active effort to change; 〜ようになる = natural/gradual change over time
〜ように = so that (purpose clause); 〜ようにする = make effort to do

common_mistakes: CONFUSION with 〜ように (purpose). '早く起きるようにする' = make effort to wake early (correct). '早く起きるように' = so that I wake early (incomplete, needs main clause). Also: 〜ようにする implies trying but not always succeeding - don't use for guaranteed results.

meaning: come to be; reach state where; become able to
detailed_explanation: Natural/gradual change over time. NOT an effort pattern (that's 〜ようにする). Indicates reaching a new state or ability through time/experience.
conjugation_rules: Verb (dictionary form/potential/negative) + ようになる / い-adjective + ようになる / な-adjective + な + ようになる

usage_examples.japanese: 日本に来てから、毎日お茶を飲むようになった
usage_examples.reading: にほんにきてから、まいにちおちゃをのむようになった
usage_examples.meaning: Since coming to Japan, I've come to drink tea every day
usage_examples.alternative: 〜ようにした (would imply conscious effort, not natural)

usage_examples.japanese: 毎日練習したら、泳げるようになった
usage_examples.reading: まいにちれんしゅうしたら、およげるようになった
usage_examples.meaning: After practicing every day, I became able to swim

usage_examples.japanese: 最近、涙が出ないようになった
usage_examples.reading: さいきん、なみだがでないようになった
usage_examples.meaning: Lately, I've reached a point where I don't cry

nuance_notes: 〜ようになる = natural change over time, often unintentional. Different from 〜ようにする (active effort). Usually appears with てから (since), 毎日 (every day), 最近 (lately) - time indicators. Can express: ability acquisition, habit formation, emotional/physical change.

related_patterns.key_difference: 〜ようになる = natural/gradual change; 〜ようにする = active effort/trying
〜ようになる = natural change over time; 〜ことになる = decided/scheduled outcome (often external decision)
〜くなる = become (adjective); 〜ようになる = come to (verb state)

common_mistakes: Using 〜ようになる when you mean 〜ようにする (effort). '毎日勉強するようになった' = naturally came to study (no effort implied). If you tried hard, use 〜ようにした. Also: Don't use for sudden changes - ようになる implies gradual process.

meaning: it is decided that; come to be that; end up doing
detailed_explanation: Scheduled outcome, external decision, or natural consequence. Often passive/result-oriented. Different from 〜ようになる (gradual change).

usage_examples.japanese: 来月、東京に転勤することになった
usage_examples.reading: らいげつ、とうきょうにてんきんすることになった
usage_examples.meaning: It has been decided that I'll transfer to Tokyo next month

usage_examples.japanese: 毎日残業することになっている
usage_examples.reading: まいにちざんぎょうすることになっている
usage_examples.meaning: It has been arranged that I work overtime every day

usage_examples.japanese: よく考えたら、留学しないことにした
usage_examples.reading: よくかんがえたら、りゅうがくしないことにした
usage_examples.meaning: After thinking carefully, I decided not to study abroad
usage_examples.nuance: Personal decision (ことにする vs ことになる)
usage_examples.alternative: 〜ことにした (I decided) vs 〜ことになった (it was decided)

nuance_notes: 〜ことになる = external decision/scheduled outcome (often passive). 〜ことにする = I decide (active). 〜ことになっている = currently arranged/ongoing arrangement. 〜ことになった = became the situation (past). Often used for company decisions, natural consequences, or unavoidable outcomes.

related_patterns.key_difference: 〜ことになる = it is decided/external; 〜ことにする = I decide/active choice
〜ようになる = gradual natural change; 〜ことになる = decided/scheduled outcome
〜ことになった = became; 〜ことになっている = currently arranged as ongoing state

common_mistakes: Passive/active confusion. Use 〜ことになる for external decisions (company, fate). Use 〜ことにする for your own decisions. Also: Don't use 〜ことになる for physical changes (×強くなることになった) - that's 〜ようになる.

meaning: decide to; make it a rule to; choose to
detailed_explanation: Active decision pattern. Personal commitment or definitive choice. Stronger and more decisive than 〜ようにする (effort).

usage_examples.japanese: 毎朝６時に起きることにした
usage_examples.reading: まいあさろくじにおきることにした
usage_examples.meaning: I've decided to wake up at 6 every morning
usage_examples.alternative: 〜ようにしている (would imply trying, less definitive)

usage_examples.japanese: お酒をやめることにしました
usage_examples.reading: おさけをやめることにしました
usage_examples.meaning: I've decided to quit drinking
usage_examples.alternative: 〜やめようと思う (thinking of quitting, less committed)

usage_examples.japanese: 週末は家族と過ごすことにしている
usage_examples.reading: しゅうまつはかぞくとすごすことにしている
usage_examples.meaning: I make it a rule to spend weekends with family
usage_examples.alternative: 〜ようにしている (more effort-based, less rule-like)

nuance_notes: 〜ことにする = definitive decision (one-time). 〜ことにしている = ongoing rule/habit (continuous). Stronger than 〜ようにする. Often used for life decisions, rules, policies. Carries weight of commitment.

related_patterns.key_difference: 〜ことにする = I decide; 〜ことになる = it is decided/external
〜ことにする = definitive decision/rule; 〜ようにする = effort/trying (may not always succeed)
〜と思う = thinking of doing; 〜ことにする = have decided (stronger commitment)

common_mistakes: Using 〜ことにする for tentative intentions (too strong). Use 〜ようにする or 〜と思う for trying/considering. Also: 〜ことにしている vs 〜ようにしている - former is established rule, latter is ongoing effort.

meaning: I hear that; apparently; it is said that
detailed_explanation: Reported information pattern - relaying what you heard from others. NOT personal observation (that's 〜そうです appearance). Different grammatical rules.
conjugation_rules: Verb (plain form/past/negative) + そうだ / い-adjective + そうだ / な-adjective + だ + そうだ / Noun + だ + そうだ

usage_examples.japanese: 田中さんは結婚するそうだ
usage_examples.reading: たなかさんはけっこんするそうだ
usage_examples.meaning: I hear that Tanaka is getting married

usage_examples.japanese: 雨が降るそうですから、傘を持って行きましょう
usage_examples.reading: あめがふるそうですから、かさをもっていきましょう
usage_examples.meaning: I hear it's going to rain, so let's bring umbrellas
usage_examples.alternative: 〜みたいです (seems like, more personal observation)

usage_examples.japanese: 先生は今日いないそうです
usage_examples.reading: せんせいはきょういないそうです
usage_examples.meaning: I hear the teacher isn't here today

nuance_notes: 〜そうだ (hearsay) = relaying information from source. NO conjugation before そう - use plain form directly. Different from 〜そうです (appearance) which conjugates differently (drop い/な). 〜そうだ often with 〜によると (according to).

related_patterns.key_difference: 〜そうだ (hearsay) = plain form + そうだ; 〜そうです (appearance) = stem form, い→さ, な→そう
〜そうだ = I heard; 〜らしい = seems/apparently (more uncertain, based on indirect evidence)
〜そうだ = casual hearsay; 〜とのことだ = reported speech formal

common_mistakes: CRITICAL: Confusing 〜そうだ (hearsay) with 〜そうです (appearance/looks like). '降るそうだ' = I hear it will rain (hearsay). '降りそうだ' = It looks like it's going to rain (appearance). Different conjugation! Also: Don't use 〜ないそうだ → use 〜ないそうです (hearsay with ない stays as ない).

detailed_explanation: Highly versatile: hearsay with uncertainty, inference based on evidence, or characteristic quality (-ish). N3's most ambiguous pattern - context determines meaning.
conjugation_rules: Verb (plain/past/negative) + らしい / い-adjective + らしい / な-adjective + らしい / Noun + らしい

usage_examples.japanese: 田中さんは帰ったらしい
usage_examples.reading: たなかさんはかえったらしい
usage_examples.meaning: It seems/apparently Tanaka has gone home
usage_examples.nuance: Inference based on indirect evidence (his bag is gone)

usage_examples.japanese: 彼は子供らしくない
usage_examples.reading: かれはこどもらしくない
usage_examples.meaning: He's not childlike/unchildish
usage_examples.nuance: Characteristic quality - not typical of children

usage_examples.japanese: 雨が降るらしいですよ
usage_examples.reading: あめがふるらしいですよ
usage_examples.meaning: I hear/It seems it will rain

nuance_notes: THREE FUNCTIONS: (1) Hearsay (with uncertainty) - weaker than 〜そうだ (2) Inference - based on evidence, not direct observation (3) Characteristic - childlike, spring-like (〜らしい). When attached to nouns = characteristic. When attached to sentences = hearsay/inference. 〜らしいです softens statement.

related_patterns.key_difference: 〜そうだ = I heard (more certain); 〜らしい = apparently (more uncertain/indirect)
〜みたい = personal observation 'looks like'; 〜らしい = indirect evidence/apparently
〜らしい = characteristic/proper quality; 〜っぽい = tendency/slang -ish (often negative)

common_mistakes: Ambiguity confusion. '彼女は学生らしい' = (a) She's apparently a student (hearsay) (b) She's student-like (characteristic). Context determines. Also: Don't use for direct observation - that's 〜そうです (appearance).

detailed_explanation: Simile/inference pattern. Casual but versatile. Can be used for appearance-based inference or comparison. More certain than 〜らしい.
conjugation_rules: Verb (plain/past/negative) + みたい / い-adjective + みたい / な-adjective + みたい / Noun + みたい

usage_examples.japanese: あの雲は雨が降るみたいだ
usage_examples.reading: あのくもはあめがふるみたいだ
usage_examples.meaning: Those clouds look like they'll bring rain

usage_examples.japanese: 彼はまるで子供みたいに騒ぐ
usage_examples.reading: かれはまるでこどみたいにさわぐ
usage_examples.meaning: He makes noise just like a child

usage_examples.japanese: 夢みたいな体験だった
usage_examples.reading: ゆめみたいなたいけんだった
usage_examples.meaning: It was a dream-like experience

nuance_notes: 〜みたい = casual simile and inference. More certain than 〜らしい (apparently). Less formal than 〜よう/〜のよう. Often with まるで (just like). 〜みたいだ = it seems/looks like. 〜みたいな = like/similar (adjective). 〜みたいに = like/similar manner (adverb).

related_patterns.key_difference: 〜みたい = casual 'like'; 〜ように = formal/literary 'as if'
〜みたい = personal observation 'looks like'; 〜らしい = indirect evidence 'apparently'
〜そうです = immediate visual appearance; 〜みたい = general similarity/inference

common_mistakes: Overusing 〜みたい in formal situations - it's casual. Use 〜ように in business/writing. Also: 〜みたい conjugates differently from 〜そう - don't drop い for adjectives (×高そう → 高いみたい).

detailed_explanation: Excess pattern - beyond appropriate amount. Criticism or objective measurement. Different from 〜過ぎない (not too much).
conjugation_rules: Verb (stem form) + すぎる / い-adjective (drop い) + すぎる / な-adjective (drop な) + すぎる

usage_examples.japanese: 昨日は飲み過ぎた
usage_examples.reading: きのうはのみすぎた
usage_examples.meaning: I drank too much yesterday

usage_examples.japanese: このカレーは辛過ぎて食べられない
usage_examples.reading: このカレーはからすぎてたべられない
usage_examples.meaning: This curry is too spicy to eat

usage_examples.japanese: 静か過ぎて変な感じがする
usage_examples.reading: しずかすぎてへんなかんじがする
usage_examples.meaning: It's so quiet it feels strange

nuance_notes: 〜すぎる = beyond appropriate/comfortable limit. Often implies criticism, regret, or problem. 〜過ぎ (noun form) = the excess itself. 〜すぎて = so much that (result). 〜すぎると = if too much (warning). Can be objective measurement or subjective complaint.

related_patterns.key_difference: 〜過ぎる = too much; 〜過ぎない = not too much / appropriate
飲み過ぎた (verb) vs 飲み過ぎ (noun: excess drinking)
〜すぎる = did too much; 〜すぎると = if you do too much (warning)

common_mistakes: Conjugation errors: 食べる→食べすぎる (stem: 食べ), 高い→高すぎる (drop い), 静か→静かすぎる (drop な). NOT dictionary form! Also: Don't use for positive excess in formal contexts - can sound like complaint.

detailed_explanation: Quality/characteristic patterns - describing inherent ease or difficulty. Different from ability (〜ことができる). Describes object/action quality.
conjugation_rules: Verb (stem form) + やすい / Verb (stem form) + にくい

usage_examples.japanese: このペンは書きやすい
usage_examples.reading: このぺんはかきやすい
usage_examples.meaning: This pen writes easily/well
usage_examples.nuance: Inherent quality of the object - easy to write with

usage_examples.japanese: この本は読みにくい
usage_examples.reading: このほんはよみにくい
usage_examples.meaning: This book is difficult to read
usage_examples.nuance: Inherent difficulty - content, font, or style

usage_examples.japanese: その店は見つけにくい
usage_examples.reading: そのみせはみつけにくい
usage_examples.meaning: That shop is hard to find

nuance_notes: 〜やすい/〜にくい = inherent quality describing object/action. Different from 〜ことができる (ability) or 〜のが好き (preference). These describe HOW the action performs - smooth, difficult, etc. Common in product reviews, instructions, descriptions.

related_patterns.key_difference: 〜にくい = physically/technically difficult; 〜がたい = emotionally difficult (can't bear to)
〜しやすい = tends to easily (more emphatic); 〜やすい = easy to do
〜にくい = difficult quality; 〜づらい = burdensome/hard to bear doing

common_mistakes: Confusing with 〜ことができる (ability). '読むことができない' = can't read (lack of skill). '読みにくい' = hard to read (bad font/content). Different! Also: Don't use for emotional difficulty (use 〜がたい). Stem form only - not dictionary form!
